narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, you've shown determination in pursuing your dream of becoming a lawyer, and we appreciate the strengths in your background, like your middle-family income and choice to study full-time. The admissions model we use predicts your likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, and unfortunately, it estimated a 59.7% chance that you would not pass, which is why your application was rejected—schools rely on this to ensure high success rates for their students. The most influential factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your Year 3 school grade decile, which placed you in the bottom 10% of your peers, far below the typical student's middle-of-the-pack standing. Your Year 1 grade decile, also in the lowest 10% compared to the average around the middle, added to this concern as the next strongest signal of potential struggle. On a positive note, your LSAT score of 33, while below the average of about 37, helped pull the prediction toward success more than most factors. Similarly, your undergraduate GPA of 3.00, a bit under the typical 3.21, still offered some reassurance to the model. Your middle-income family background and full-time study plan provided smaller boosts toward passing, aligning with what's common for successful students. Overall, your early and later high school grades stood out as the biggest hurdles when compared to typical applicants who pass the bar, outweighing your other solid attributes. This doesn't define your potential—many overcome similar starts through targeted improvement. We encourage you to retake the LSAT to boost your score, focus on strengthening your academic performance in any upcoming courses, and consider programs offering extra bar prep support for students like you. You have the resilience to retry, and with these steps, you can build a stronger profile for future applications. Keep pushing forward; your story is far from over.
